segments.s124-g65: بزد is past tense (3rd singular) of زدن, here ‘he struck/beat’ with کوس (war‑drum) as its object. The coordinating و links that action to برداشت, where برداشت (past 3rd sg of برداشتن) means “took up / set in motion”; از + نیمروز gives the time/place of movement: ‘from/at noon.’ Word order is verb — object — conjunction — verb — prepositional phrase, typical of Persian poetic inversion.
segments.s124-g66: سپه is the head noun ‘army, host’. The following cluster شاد دل شاه گیتی‌فروز is a chain of modifiers/appositions: شاد دل = ‘joyous‑hearted’ (an adjective phrase), شاه = ‘the king’ (the possessor/title), and گیتی‌فروز is an epithet ‘world‑illuminating.’ Together they specify whose army it is: ‘the army of the joyous‑hearted, world‑illuminating king.’
segments.s124-g67: همه is the plural subject ‘all/everyone’. بر + سپرها is the locative prepositional phrase ‘on the shields’. نبشتند is past 3rd‑plural ‘they wrote/inscribed’ (note classical poetic form), and نام is the direct object ‘name’ placed after the verb for emphatic/poetic word order: ‘they wrote name(s) on the shields.’
segments.s124-g68: بجوشید is a past/inchoative verbal form meaning ‘boiled/ seethed / gushed’ used figuratively here; شمشیرها (the plural subject) are placed after the verb (poetic inversion). در نیام is the locative phrase ‘in the scabbard’. The whole clause pictures swords ‘seething’ inside their sheaths — i.e. eager to be drawn.
segments.s124-g69: سپه را: سپه ‘army’ marked by را as a definite/direct object (classical topical/definite marking here). ز is the poetic preposition ‘from’, هامون is ‘the plain’, به دریا = ‘into/to the sea’ (به + noun indicating motion into/ toward), and کشید is the past 3rd‑singular of کشیدن ‘drew/moved’ — so ‘he moved the army from the plain into the sea.’
segments.s124-g70: بدان سو is a fused adverbial phrase ‘to that side / toward that direction’ (بدان = به + آن in classical/poetic spelling, سو = side/direction). کجا is the interrogative ‘where’ (here rhetorical), and دشمن آمد پدید is a two‑part predicate: آمد ‘came’ (past) plus پدید ‘forth/appeared’, together ‘appeared/came into view.’ The clause therefore asks, ‘where did the enemy appear?’
segments.s124-g71: بی‌اندازه is an adverb meaning ‘without limit/excessively/countless’ modifying the verb. کشتی و زورق are the coordinated direct objects ‘ships and boats’. بساخت (classical past of ساختن with poetic prefix) means ‘he/they made/built (many)’, here ‘built countless ships and boats.’ Word order places the adverb before the objects for emphasis.
segments.s124-g72: برآشفت is a past/inchoative verb meaning roughly ‘rose/was launched’ (literally ‘became agitated/uplifted’) and is used here of vessels being put to sea. و = ‘and’. بر آب is the prepositional phrase ‘onto the water’. لشکر is ‘the army/host’, and نشاخت is an archaic poetic past form meaning ‘sat/settled/took position’ (here: ‘the army embarked / took to the water’). The clause thus describes the launching and embarkation of the host.
segments.s124-g81: خبر شد is an impersonal/past construction meaning 'news happened/reached' (literally 'news became'); بدیشان is the dative 'to them' marking the experiencers; که introduces the reported content (a subordinate clause) and is followed by the subject phrase کاووس شاه 'King Kavus.'
segments.s124-g82: برآمد is a past tense verb 'emerged/issued forth'; ز is the poetic ablative 'from'; آب is 'water'; زره 'armor' here describes his state (he came out armored); با سپاه = 'with an army' (با marking accompaniment). The whole clause states how/with whom he came out.
segments.s124-g83: هم‌آواز is an adverb/adjective 'in unison' or 'of one accord'; گشتند is past plural 'became/went' (here 'became [one]'); یک با دگر literally 'one with another' — a poetic phrase meaning they joined or agreed. Word order places the adverbial first, then the verb and the phrase emphasizing unity.
segments.s124-g84: سپه را uses را to mark the army as the definite/ topicalized element; سوی بربر 'toward the Berbers' gives direction; آمد گذر is an inverted/poetic sequence where گذر ('passage' / 'the act of passing') with آمد ('came' / 'began') expresses that their passage/advance toward the Berbers took place (i.e. they set off).
segments.s124-g85: یکی گشت here uses یکی ('one' / 'a certain (thing)') with گشت ('became/was made') to introduce that a number/contingent came into being; چندان 'so many' quantifies the following noun; یل تیغ‌زن is a compound 'hero(s) who strike with the sword' (swordsmen). The phrase describes the emergence of numerous fighters.
segments.s124-g86: به + place (بربرستان) marks motion into the land of the Berbers; در شدند is an older/periphrastic way to express 'entered' or 'became in' (در + شدن = 'to enter'); انجمن 'assembly/meeting' is the result: they gathered. The clause thus says they went into Berber-land and assembled.
segments.s124-g87: سپاهی is the noun 'army/host' and که introduces a relative clause describing it; the list دریا و صحرا و کوه ('sea and plain and mountain') are the places affected and are the objects/extent introduced for the following clause. Grammatically, که ties these places to the following predicate about the army's travelling.
segments.s124-g88: شد is the past copula 'became' (here forming 'became weary'); از marks the cause/means 'from' (i.e. 'from the horseshoes'); نعل اسپان 'the horseshoes of the steeds' (نعل = horseshoe, اسپان = horses); ایشان is the possessive/third-person plural 'their'; ستوه is an adjective 'weary/afflicted.' The older word order places شد before the predicate adjective ستوه.
segments.s124-g9: چنین هم = an adverbial phrase 'thus / even so' placed before the subject for emphasis. گرازان is the subject 'the boars'. به + بربر شد(ند) uses به with شدن to mark a change of state 'become into/turned into' (poetic word order: adverb — subject — predicate).
segments.s124-g10: جهانجوی is a plural subject 'world-seekers / ambitious men' (noun). با تخت و افسر شدند is the predicate 'became/assumed (with) throne and helmet' where با marks accompaniment/possession ('with') and شد(ند) is the past plural of شدن 'became' — i.e. they were installed/armed with throne and helmet.
segments.s124-g11: شه بربرستان is a nominal phrase 'the king of Berberstan' (شه = king + place-name). بیاراست is a past tense verb from آراستن 'to adorn/array/prepare' taking جنگ 'war' as its object; classical Persian often uses Subject–Object–Verb order.
segments.s124-g12: زمانه 'the age / the times' is the subject; دگرگونه‌تر is the comparative adjective 'more different / changed in kind' and شد is the past 'became.' به رنگ is a prepositional phrase 'in (its) color/appearance' following the verb to specify in what respect the change happened.
segments.s124-g13: سپاهی is the subject 'a host/army'; بیامد is the simple past 'came' (S V). ز is the poetic preposition 'from' marking origin (ز بربر = from the Berbers) and به رزم 'to battle' uses به + noun to indicate purpose or direction 'into/for battle.'
segments.s124-g14: که introduces a relative clause ('which / that / for it') modifying سپاهی. برخاست is past 'rose / set out'; از marks source 'from' and لشکر شاه بزم is a genitive chain 'the army/host of the king's feast/camp' (لشکرِ شاهِ بزم). The clause explains the origin of that host.
segments.s124-g15: هوا is 'the air' (or atmosphere). گفتی here is a parenthetical/poetic usage equivalent to 'one might say' or 'so to speak.' از نیزه marks source 'from the spears' and چون بیشه گشت is a simile 'became like a thicket' (چون = 'like', بیشه = 'grove/thicket', گشت = past 'became').
segments.s124-g16: خور is the sun as subject. از گرد اسپان is a prepositional phrase where گرد likely means the dust/whirl of (گرد = 'dust' here) around the horses (اسپان). پراندیشه is an adjectival/state word meaning 'troubled / anxious / greatly mindful' and گشت is 'became' — i.e. the sun was made to seem disturbed or obscured by the horses' dust.
segments.s124-g25: Two past verbs are placed before their subject in poetic inversion: برآویخت (past 3rd sg) "rose up" and بدرید (past 3rd sg) "tore". The subject قلب سپاه (the army's core) follows the verbs: literally, “it rose up and tore the core of the army.” This fronting of verbs is common in epic diction to heighten action.
segments.s124-g26: دمان از پس is a prepositional phrase meaning "(his) train/skirt following behind" (دمان = hem/ train), از پس = "from/after/behind." اندر همی رفت شاه places the verb phrase (همی رفت, imperfective/past-continuous poetic form "went/was going") before the subject شاه (the king) — a typical word order in older Persian where the subject comes at the end. همی here marks ongoing or habitual past action.
segments.s124-g27: تو گفتی is the 2nd-person past "you said," introducing the reported clause that follows. ز is the poetic partitive/preposition "from/of," so ز بربر سواری نماند reads "of the barbarians no horseman remained" — نماند is the past-negative/existential "remained not / was left no." Note: the provided lemma-senses for گفتی do not list the verbal sense; the segment functions as the speech-verb "you said."
segments.s124-g28: به گرد اندرون is a locative phrase "around/round the inner (part)" (به گرد = around; اندرون = inside). نیزه‌داری نماند is another existential negative: نیزه‌داری (spear-bearing / lancers) + نماند "there remained none." The noun comes before نماند, which serves as the predicate "were left/not left."
segments.s124-g29: به شهر اندرون is a locative fronting meaning "into/within the city." هرکه introduces a free relative "whoever," followed by the compound description بد سالخورد (بد + سالخورد) meaning literally "badly aged" i.e. "grown old and infirm/weak." The adjective phrase follows the relative pronoun, a common order in Persian.
segments.s124-g30: چو (when) + برگشته (returned, a past participle) sets a temporal clause, followed by دیدند (they saw) as the main verb. باد here most likely denotes the noun "wind," and نبرد appears in the text where context suggests a negative/result clause ("had not carried [it]") — however the lemma senses supplied for نبرد are nominal and do not fit a verbal reading, so its exact syntactic role is uncertain in the provided senses. Overall the structure is: "When they returned, they saw that [the wind had not carried it away]."
segments.s124-g31: همه (everyone) is the subject, پیش کاووس شاه is a locative phrase "in the presence of Kavus the king" (پیش = before/in the presence of), and آمدند is the past-plural verb "they came." Word order places the subject first and the locative complement before the verb, with the verb ending the clause.
segments.s124-g32: جگرخسته و پرگناه are two predicate adjectives placed before the verb آمدند; together they modify the subject (they) and describe the manner/state of their coming: "heart‑weary and full of guilt." آمدند repeats the past-plural "they came," giving an emphatic, repeated arrival.
segments.s124-g49: سپَه (the army) is the subject; ره (way/route) is a direct object/accusative noun meaning 'the road' (poetic use instead of a phrase like 'راه را'); سوی زابلستان is a prepositional phrase 'toward Zabulistan'; کشید is the simple past of کشیدن used here intransitively/figuratively 'to take/move (one's) way' — a common poetic collocation meaning 'marched/advanced'. Word order is typical Persian with the verb at the end; the lack of the particle را is normal in classical verse.
segments.s124-g50: به introduces a goal 'to/for' and مهمانی is the noun 'banquet/feast'; پور دستان is a personal-name construction (پور = 'son of' + دستان as the name) functioning as possessor of the feast ('Pour-Dastan's banquet'); کشید again is past 'drew/took (their way)' used for 'went to'. The whole clause means 'they went to Pour-Dastan's feast.'
segments.s124-g51: ببد is an archaic/past-form verb meaning 'was' or 'stayed'; شاه is the subject 'the king'; یک ماه is a duration 'one month'; در + نیمروز here denotes location/region 'in Nimruz' (the region rather than 'noon' in this context). The phrase says the king spent one month in Nimruz.
segments.s124-g52: گهی 'sometimes' is an adverb; رود here is an archaic/persianized present/subjunctive form of رفتن ('he would go/ride' or 'he went out') rather than the noun 'river' — this is a poetic verb form; و می‌خواست is 'and he wanted/wished'; گه 'at times/sometimes' repeats the adverbial sense; باز is 'hawk/falcon' and یوز is 'cheetah' (both hunting animals desired for sport). The clause portrays intermittent outings and a wish for hunting birds/animals.
segments.s124-g53: برین here is a fused form of بر + این ('on/unto this' or 'to this'); برنیامد is a negative past construction meaning 'did not come about / did not succeed'; بسی روزگار is a temporal phrase 'much time / many days' (bسی = much/many, روزگار = time/days). The segment says that for a long time the desired event did not occur.
segments.s124-g54: که introduces the subordinate clause 'that/for'; بر here means 'on/upon'; گوشه گلستان is the locative phrase 'a corner of the rose-garden' (گوشه = corner, گلستان = rose-garden); رست is past of رستن 'to grow/sprout'; خار is 'thorn'. The clause literally: 'that on the corner of the rose-garden a thorn grew.'
segments.s124-g55: کس with the following negative verb expresses 'no one' here; از introduces the source/means 'by/from'; آزمایش is 'trial/ordeal'; نیابد is a negative present/past-form meaning 'does not obtain/does not attain' (classical usage); جواز is 'permission/right' — together: 'No one obtains the right by trial' (i.e., one cannot be granted status without being tested).
segments.s124-g56: نشیب 'descent/low point' is the noun subject of the clause that follows; آیدش is a cliticized verb 'comes to him' (آید + -ش 'to him'); چون here means 'how' (introducing a rhetorical question); شود is the verb 'become'/'be' in the subjunctive/indicative sense; بر فراز is a prepositional phrase 'on the heights/summit'. Together the question asks rhetorically: 'when a low point comes to him, how can he be on the summit?' indicating one must endure trial to stay high.
segments.s124-g57: چو + past verb شد introduces a temporal clause 'when'. کار گیتی is the subject phrase ('the affairs/work of the world') placed after the verb in poetic word order. بران راستی is a locative/goal phrase ('on/to that rightness/uprightness') modifying how the world's affairs were set.
segments.s124-g58: پدید آمد is a compound verbal phrase meaning 'appeared / came to pass.' از marks the source/possession and introduces the genitive phrase تازیان کاستی ('a deficiency/shortage of Arabs'); the noun کاستی is the notional subject/thing that 'appeared.'
segments.s124-g59: یکی functions as an indefinite 'a certain' or 'one (man)'. The phrases با گهر and با گنج و نام are instrumental/possessive modifiers ('with a gem', 'with treasure and name/fame') that qualify مرد ('man, hero') placed between them in poetic order.
segments.s124-g60: درفشی is the direct object ('a banner/standard') placed before the verb in inverted poetic order. برافراخت is the past tense 'he hoisted/erected.' از مصر و شام is a prepositional phrase indicating origin ('from Egypt and Sham'), with و simply joining the two place-names.
segments.s124-g61: ز (poetic) marks relation to Kāvus ('from/with respect to Kavus'). کی here functions as an indefinite/relative 'those who.' روی is the object 'face' and برتافتند is a past verb meaning roughly 'they turned away / bore/put up with' in this idiomatic poetic phrase 'روی برتافتند' (i.e., they averted their faces).
segments.s124-g62: در introduces the state 'in' which follows. کهتری خوار is a collocation meaning 'lowliness/being lesser and despised' (کهتری = inferiority, خوار = despised), and بگذاشتند is the past verb 'they left/placed,' with the object implied (i.e., they left him in that state).
segments.s124-g63: چو آمد begins a temporal clause 'when ... came.' به شاه جهان is a dative phrase 'to the king of the world' (epithet), and آگهی ('news/information') is the thing that arrived; the verb precedes the indirect object and noun in this poetic ordering.
segments.s124-g64: که introduces the reported-content clause 'that ...'. انباز is an obscure or archaic term here (possibly a proper name or rare noun) and is marked as uncertain/obsolete in the lexical list; دارد is the present/past-habitual sense 'has/holds', and به شاهنشهی ('to/for the imperial kingship') uses به to mark the office/role held. The clause therefore reports that 'Anbaz holds/assumes the imperial rule.'
segments.s124-g41: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. آمد is simple past 3rd-singular 'came/reached'. The noun phrase بر + شهر مکران + گذر is compressed: بر here = 'to/toward', شهر مکران = 'the city of Makran', and گذر ('passage/party') is the thing whose arrival is being described; word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect (verb before the following noun-phrase).
segments.s124-g42: سوی + place is a directional prepositional phrase 'toward'. کوه قاف is the destination (Mount Qāf). آمد repeats the past verb 'came/moved'. و is the coordinating particle 'and'. باختر is an adverb/noun meaning 'west' or 'westward' and here functions as an additional directional adverb; the clause is compacted in epic style.
segments.s124-g43: چو 'when' again begins a temporal clause. آگاهی here means 'news/information' (an arrived report). آمد is past 'arrived'. بریشان is بر + the enclitic شان 'to them' (preposition with 3rd-plural clitic) meaning 'to them'. ز شاه is the source 'from the king' placed after the object in this poetic order.
segments.s124-g44: نیایش‌کنان is an adverbial present/participial form meaning 'praying' or 'in prayer' (the -ان ending gives an adverbial/continuous sense). برگرفتند is past 3rd-plural 'they took up/took' (برگرفتن used here with راه as its object). راه is the direct object 'the road/the way'. The participial phrase modifies the verb: 'they, praying, took the road.'
segments.s124-g45: پذیره here denotes a reception/welcoming. شدندش is a compound in which شدند (they became/did) takes the accusative clitic -ش ('him'), yielding the idiomatic meaning 'they received/welcomed him' in this context (poetic/passive-adjunct style). همه مهتران is the subject 'all the chiefs/elders' (مهتران = chiefs/important men) placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s124-g46: به سر برنهادند is a verbal phrase: به سر = 'to the head/they raised to their heads' and برنهادند is past 3rd-plural 'they raised/lifted' (برنهادن). باژ گران is a noun + adjective 'heavy/loud cry' (باژ = cry/noise, گران = heavy/weighty used as an intensifier), describing what they uttered as they raised their heads; adjective follows noun in Persian poetic order.
segments.s124-g47: چو 'when' marks the time clause. فرمان گزیدند is past 'they chose/decided the command/order' (گزیدن = to choose). بگرفت راه is a light-verb construction: بگرفت (took/seized) + راه (the road) meaning 'they took up the road / set out on the road'. The prefix ب- on گرفت forms a perfective past action.
segments.s124-g48: بی‌آزار is an adverbial compound meaning 'without harm / harmlessly / without trouble' placed before the verb for emphasis. رفتند is past 3rd-plural 'they went/departed'. شاه و سپاه ('the king and the army') are the coordinated subject placed after the verb (poetic inversion): 'the king and his host departed unharmed.'
segments.s124-g97: گفتی is the 2nd-person past tense 'you said'. جهان is the theme/quote (what was said): 'the world'. از ... است is a copular construction meaning 'is made of / consists of'; تیغ 'sword' and جوشن 'cuirass/armor' are the things that form the world. وز here functions as a poetic connector joining تیغ and جوشن (similar to 'and/from ... and ...').
segments.s124-g98: ستاره is the subject 'a star'. ز نوک سنان is a prepositional phrase 'from the tips of spears' (ز = 'from', نوک = 'tip', سنان = 'spears'). روشن است is the predicate 'is bright/gleaming' (است = copula). Word order is Subject – (prep phrase) – Predicate, typical in poetry.
segments.s124-g99: ز بس introduces a causal/partitive idea 'from/through so much' or 'because of so much'. خود here is an intensifier / 'own' (here strengthening 'much' or indicating 'their very'), and زرین 'golden' describes what is abundant; the phrase زرین و زرین سپر repeats/links golden items and the golden shield (و = 'and'). The whole phrase names the gleaming, golden equipment responsible for what follows.
segments.s124-g100: به گردن is the location 'on (the) neck' (به = 'on/to', گردن = 'neck'). برآورده is a past participle/inflected verb meaning 'put/raised/placed' (here 'was put/was lifted on'), and رخشان modifies تبر as 'shining, radiant axe' (رخشان تبر = 'the bright axe'). The participle agrees idiomatically with the subject implied earlier (the warriors).
segments.s124-g101: تو گفتی again is 'you said' (2nd-person past). زمین شد is 'the earth became' (شد = past of 'become'). سپهر روان is the predicate 'a moving/flowing sky' (سپهر = sky/heaven, روان used adjectivally = 'flowing/animated'), so the clause reports the speaker's words: 'you said the earth became a flowing heaven.'
segments.s124-g102: همی + present-stem here marks an ongoing/habitual action; همی بارد means 'it keeps falling / it rains down.' از introduces the source 'from' (از تیغ هندی), تیغ هندی 'Indian swords' names the falling objects, and روان again qualifies those swords as 'flowing/rapid' (a poetic epithet). Note: بارد here is a verbal form ('falls/rains'), not the adjective 'cold'.
segments.s124-g103: ز introduces cause/agent 'from/because of'. مغفر here is a noun meaning 'covering/veil' (not 'forgiveness' in this context) and هوا is 'the air/atmosphere'. گشت is the past of 'become/turn' ('became'), and چون + سندروس is a simile 'like sandalwood' (سندروس = fragrant wood). So: 'Because of the covering, the air became like sandalwood.'
segments.s124-g104: زمین 'the earth' is the subject. سر به سر is an idiomatic expression meaning 'throughout / from end to end / entirely' (literally 'head to head'). تیره is an adjective 'dark/black', and چون آبنوس is a simile 'like ebony'. The clause states that the earth was wholly dark as ebony.
segments.s124-g33: که is a subordinating/complementizer introducing the content of the speech ('that'). ما is the explicit subject 'we'. شاه را marks 'the king' with the definite object/topic marker را (here indicating the person to whom servitude is owed). چاکر و بنده‌ایم is the predication: two nominal predicates joined by و ('and'), with بنده‌ایم carrying the 1st-person-plural ending -ایم 'we are' (classical present/copula), so the whole means 'we are the king's chākar (retainer) and bende (servant)'.
segments.s124-g34: همه 'everyone' is a universal subject/quantifier. باژ را uses را to mark باژ (here 'the summons/call/cry') as the definite target; the phrase گردن افگنده‌ایم is a compound with the past participle افگنده plus the 1pl ending -ایم, used idiomatically to mean 'we have cast/bowed our necks' i.e. 'we have submitted'. The tense/aspect is a completed/resultative action (classical poetic usage).
segments.s124-g35: به جای 'in place of / instead of' is a prepositional phrase (به + جای) governing درم 'dirham(s)'. زر و گوهر is the coordinated direct object ('gold and jewels'), and دهیم is the 1st-person-plural present/jussive form of دادن 'to give' used here as a pledge/intentional action 'we will give' or 'we give'.
segments.s124-g36: سپاسی 'a thanks/tribute' is the direct object; ز is the poetical preposition 'from' introducing گنجور 'the treasury/treasure-house' as source. بر سر is a locative phrase 'upon the head', and نهیم is the 1st-person-plural present/jussive of نهادن 'to place/set' meaning 'we place / we will place'. The clause expresses an intended ceremonial placement of tribute.
segments.s124-g37: ببخشود is the 3rd-person-singular past tense 'he forgave' (classical perfect). The following noun کاووس is the subject (verb–subject order is common in epic/poetic style). و links the next verbal action; بنواختشان is composed of the preterite/preterital-stem نواخت plus the 3pl object suffix -شان 'them', meaning 'he played (music) for them' or 'caused music/sound for them'.
segments.s124-g38: یکی here functions as an indefinite/quantifying element 'a certain' or 'one' (introducing a single, particular thing). راه و آیین is a coordinated object 'way and ritual/custom', modified by نو 'new'. ساختشان is the 3rd-singular past ساخت 'he made/established' with the object suffix -شان 'them' (i.e. he established these new practices for them).
segments.s124-g39: وزان is a fused particle meaning 'and from that' or 'thence'. جایگه is 'place/position'. بانگ is 'the sound/cry' and is here used with musical instruments that follow; سنج names a musical instrument (cymbal) in this context, and و درای links another instrument (drum or similar) — these are listed as the sources of the sound. The whole phrase describes musical noise issuing from that location.
segments.s124-g40: برآمد is the past tense verb 'arose/issued forth'. ابا here appears as part of the collocation introducing the lament; in this poetic context it helps frame ناله 'lament/cry' as an emanation (the lexical inventory lists a different primary sense, so this is a fossilized/poetic usage). ناله is 'lament/moan', and کره‌نای is the name of a small reed instrument (a little pipe), so the clause as a whole says that a lamenting note of the small pipe rose up.
segments.s124-g1: ازان پس ('thereupon/after that') is an adverbial phrase sequencing the action; چنین کرد is a fixed sequence meaning 'did thus/made such', where کرد is the past-tense verb 'did/made'. کاووس is the subject (the king) and رای is the object meaning 'decision/plan' — literally 'Kavus made such a decision.'
segments.s124-g2: که introduces the content clause (the thing decided). در پادشاهی is a locative phrase 'in (his) kingship/at the throne'; بجنبد is a present/jussive-form of جنبیدن meaning 'move/leave/be stirred' giving the sense 'should move' or 'would leave'; ز جای is the prepositional phrase 'from (his) place/seat.' The clause therefore states what the decision was: that he would move from his seat of kingship.
segments.s124-g3: از ایران بشد is a classical phrasing meaning 'he departed from Iran' (بشد here indicates the happening/becoming of the action). تا ... چین uses تا in the 'as far as' sense to mark the extent: 'as far as Turan and China.'
segments.s124-g4: گذر کرد is a compound verbal expression 'he passed/travelled' (گذر 'passing' + کرد 'did'), and ازان پس is a sequence adverb 'thereafter/then.' به مکران زمین is a prepositional phrase 'into the land of Makran' (مکران = the place name, زمین = 'land').
segments.s124-g5: ز مکران شد آراسته: ز introduces origin 'from Makran'; شد آراسته is 'became/got arrayed' (شد = become/happen, آراسته = 'adorned/equipped'). تا زره uses تا in the 'as far as/up to' sense: 'up to (his) armor.'
segments.s124-g6: میانها is 'among/between them' (میان + plural marker), ندید is past 'saw not' (negative form), ایچ is the emphatic 'nothing/none', and رنج از گره means 'suffering/trouble from a knot/entanglement' (از marks source/cause). Together: 'among them he saw no hardship from any entanglement.'
segments.s124-g7: پذیرفت is past 'accepted/received.' هر مهتری is 'each/any chief/elder.' باژ و ساو appear as a coordinated pair (likely proper names in the narrative) appended to 'هر مهتری' — the phrasing lists or identifies chiefs; in the poem these are names of persons or groups accepted by the ruler.
segments.s124-g8: نکرد + آزمون is a negated past construction: 'did not make/do a trial.' گاو is 'cow/ox' (the thing not put to the test); با is 'with/against' marking the adversary; شیر here is 'lion' (not 'milk') so the sense is a contest between ox and lion; تاو is a name or epithet (literally 'radiance' in the lemma list) attached to the lion. Together: 'he did not test the ox against the lion Tāw.'
segments.s124-g105: بدرید is a past, perfective verb form (here best read as 'they tore' or 'it was torn'); classical Persian often places the verb before its object. از دم گاودم is an ablative/source phrase: از 'from' + دم 'tip/edge' (here 'tip') followed by گاودم ‘cow's tail’ in a genitive/possessive relationship, so the whole phrase means 'from the tip of the cow's tail.'
segments.s124-g106: زمین آمد is a simple past clause 'the earth came/rose' (subject + past of آمدن). از سم اسپان is another source phrase 'from the hooves of the horses' (سم = hoof, اسپان = horses). به خم uses به ('to/towards/into') to indicate direction or resultant shape, خم = 'a bend/curve' — so 'rose into a curve.'
segments.s124-g107: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of.' بانگ is 'cry/crying out' and is the noun governed by ز. تبیره here follows بانگ as a modifier or name (poetic usage), so بانگ تبیره = 'the cry called Tabireh' (or 'the Tabireh-cry'). به بربرستان uses به to indicate direction/target 'toward Barbaristan.'
segments.s124-g108: تو گفتی is the 2nd-person past 'you said' (گفتی here functions as a past-tense verb introducing what was said). زمین گشت لشکرستان is a verbless/resultant clause with گشت 'became/turned' as the main verb: 'the land/ground became a لشکرستان (military camp or host).' The sequence shows reported speech or an addressed remark: 'you said (that) the land became a camp.'
segments.s124-g109: برآمد is a past verb 'arose/issued forth' and stands before its source phrase ز ایران 'from Iran.' سپه is the noun 'army/host' and is followed by instruments بوق و کوس 'horn and war-drum' — here a kind of apposition or accompaniment indicating the army came with trumpets and drums.
segments.s124-g110: برون‌رفت functions here as a verbal/nominal compound meaning 'went out/issued forth' (classical epic usage where verb-derived nouns may act as predicates); it introduces a list of persons who emerged. The following items are proper names in apposition (گرگین = Gorgin, فرهاد = Farhad, طوس = Tous), listed with و 'and'.
segments.s124-g111: وزان سوی is a compound locative/adverbial: وزان (from/of that) + سوی (side/toward) = 'on that side' or 'from that quarter.' گودرز کشواد بود is a verbless nominal phrase with بود as the past copula 'were/was,' linking the two names Goudarz and Keshvad as present on that side.
segments.s124-g112: چو here is a poetic particle meaning 'as/such as' and is used before names to list people present; the construction repeats چو for each group ('چو گیو و چو شیدوش') and closes with بود 'were' as the copula. The sequence therefore lists Giv, Shidush, and Milad as being there as well.
segments.s124-g129: نخستین is an ordinal/adjectival modifier placed before the noun (common word order in Persian poetry). سپهدار هاماوران is a title + place-name in apposition (classical Persian often omits the ezāfe); read as “the spahbed (commander) of Hamavaran.”
segments.s124-g130: بیفگند is a perfective (past) verb with the older prefixed b- (بـ) typical of classical Persian forms meaning “threw/cast.” The direct objects شمشیر و گرز گران follow the verb; و links the two nouns and گران is a postpositive adjective “heavy” modifying گرز (and plausibly the whole list).
segments.s124-g131: غمی گشت is an intransitive predication in which the noun غمی (‘a sorrow/grief’) is followed by the verb گشت (‘became/occurred’) — a common Persian idiom meaning “sorrow came/one became sorrowful.” وز here appears fused with the following clause; شاه زنهار خواست is a separate clause: شاه is the subject ‘the king’ and زنهار (here a noun) + خواست (‘asked/requested’) means ‘asked for a warning/admonition.’
segments.s124-g132: بدانست is a past tense verb meaning ‘knew/realized’ (بد‑ is the classical prefix attached to دانست). کان is an archaic/subordinating particle introducing the content clause (classically functioning like ‘that’), روزگار is the subject/predicate noun ‘time/fate,’ and بلاست is a predicative phrase (بلا + است) meaning ‘is calamity/disaster’ — here the copular sense is fused into the word.
segments.s124-g133: به پیمان is a prepositional phrase ‘by/under the pact/agreement.’ The conjunction/relative که introduces the subordinate clause. از شهر هاماوران is a prepositional phrase ‘from the city of Hamavaran’ that functions as the source/location inside that clause.
segments.s124-g134: سپهبد دهد uses دهد (3rd person singular of دادن) in the classical narrative to express what the spahbed is to do (a jussive/future sense: ‘should/gives’). The direct objects follow: ساو (here a toponym or name) and باژ گران (باژ + the postpositive adjective گران ‘heavy’); the adjective follows the noun, as typical in Persian poetry.
segments.s124-g135: ز + nouns (ز اسپ و سلیح و ز تخت و کلاه) is a poetic series of partitives/ablatives: ز means ‘from’ or ‘of’ here and introduces a list of things (اسپ ‘horse(s)’, سلیح ‘weapons’, تخت ‘throne’, کلاه ‘helmets/caps’). The repeated ز and the conjunction و coordinate the items; adjectives would normally follow the nouns if present.
segments.s124-g136: فرستد is the classical past/preterite (or a perfective narrative form) meaning ‘sent/dispatched.’ It takes a goal expressed by به نزدیک + شخص: به ‘to/toward’, نزدیک ‘near/into the presence of’, followed by کاووس شاه ‘Kavus the king’ (name + title), with the title following the name as is common in epic style.
segments.s124-g73: همانا (an emphatic particle) opens the line as 'indeed/verily'. که here is the subordinating particle 'that' linking this assertion to what follows. فرسنگ is the unit 'farsang/parasang'. بودی is the past copula 'was' (classical past), so the phrase literally says 'a farsang it was a thousand' — a word order inversion common in poetry.
segments.s124-g74: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. پای here means 'foot' / 'pace' (i.e. measuring by steps); با + راه forms the idiom 'با راه کردن/پای با راه کردن' meaning 'to measure by walking/pacing'. کردی is the past 2nd-person 'you did', used here in a conditional/hypothetical sense ('if you were to...'). شمار is the verbal noun 'count/number' functioning as the object ('counting it').
segments.s124-g75: همی + past verb (راند) is a classical/perfective marker meaning 'kept on doing' or 'continually did' — 'he kept driving/rode on'. تا here marks extent 'until'. در میان سه شهر is an adverbial phrase 'in the midst of three cities' (سه follows the noun, a common poetic order).
segments.s124-g76: ز is the archaic/poetic preposition 'from/of'. گیتی = 'the world'. برین‌گونه is a fused adverb 'in this manner/so' (بر + این‌گونه). جویند is present 3rd-plural 'they seek/search' (classical narrative often uses present for iterative actions). بهر is the preposition 'for/toward' (introducing what they seek; the object may be supplied by context).
segments.s124-g77: به دست چپش is a positional phrase 'on his left hand' — note the possessive enclitic -ش on چپ ('his left'). مصر و بربر are coordinated place-names/groups placed after the locative phrase to indicate who is on that side. به راست is the parallel adverbial 'to the right' (literally 'toward the right').
segments.s124-g78: زره can mean 'armor' or 'armored men/forces' in epic usage; در میانه 'in the middle/center' is a locative noun phrase. بر آن‌سو 'toward that side/over there' uses بر as a directional/prepositional particle. که introduces a relative clause 'which/that' and خواست is the past 3rd-singular 'he wished/desired', so the whole clause says the armored force in the centre was directed toward whatever side he desired.
segments.s124-g79: به پیش is a locative/ directional phrase 'ahead/forward'. اندرون شهر is 'inside the city' (اندرون = interior). هاماوران here is a place-name (classical toponym); in the given senses it appears as a lexical noun but functions as the city being named.
segments.s124-g80: به هر کشوری is 'from/at every country/realm' (هر modifies کشوری). در here introduces location 'in/there' followed by سپاهی گران — a noun phrase where سپاهی 'soldiers/army' is qualified by the adjective گران 'heavy/powerful' (poetic order places modifiers after the head at times). The whole means that heavy armies from every land were present.
segments.s124-g137: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. این داده باشد is a perfect/periphrastic clause: داده (given) + باشد (classical subjunctive/auxiliary meaning 'be/has been') = 'when this has been given' (i.e. 'when this was done'). برو بگذرد is a two‑verb sequence describing motion — برو (go) + بگذرد (pass) — here translated as 'he went and passed on'; the verbs follow the classical Persian tendency to place the action at the end, and the sequence conveys successive actions.
segments.s124-g138: سپاهش is سپاه (army) + enclitic ـش 'his' = 'his army'. بروبوم is an older compound meaning 'برو + بوم' = 'his land/realm' (poetic D-level word for homeland). او is the 3rd‑person pronoun 'he/him'. نسپرد is the past negative of سپردن 'to entrust/hand over' (نـ prefix for negation) = 'did not entrust'. The word order places the possessor on the noun (سپاهش) and the negative past verb last, so: 'he did not entrust his army to his land.'
segments.s124-g139: ز + گوینده بشنید is 'he heard from the speaker': ز is the poetic preposition 'from', گوینده 'the speaker', and بشنید is the 3rd singular past 'he heard'. کاووس follows as the subject (proper name). کی at the end is the interrogative/adverb 'when' (here = 'when this happened'); the clause has a somewhat inverted, compact classical order.
segments.s124-g140: برین گفتها = بر + این (on/upon + these) + گفتها (words/utterances) = 'upon these words'. پاسخ افگند is a fixed classical phrasing: پاسخ (answer) + افگند (past 'he cast/threw') meaning 'he gave/uttered an answer'. پی is an adverb 'after/straightaway' (here 'immediately'/'thereupon') and follows the verb. The verb‑final order places افگند at the end after its object phrase.
segments.s124-g141: که introduces the content clause 'that'. یکسر is an adverb meaning 'entirely/wholly' modifying همه 'all/everyone'. در پناه = 'in the protection (of)'. منید is an archaic copular form: not 'من (I)' + literal 'ید', but a classical 2nd‑person plural copula equivalent to modern هستید 'you are' (so the clause reads 'you are all entirely in my protection').
segments.s124-g142: پرستنده is a present‑participle/noun 'worshippers' or 'devotees' functioning as a predicate noun. تاج و گاه pairs 'crown and gāh'; in classical/poetic usage گاه here means 'seat/throne' rather than 'time', so تاج و گاه = 'crown and throne'. Again منید is the archaic 2nd‑person plural copula 'you are'. The phrase states a predicate: 'you are worshippers of my crown and throne.'
segments.s124-g89: نبد is an archaic/poetic negative existence verb (≃ نبود) meaning “there was not.” The phrase شیر درنده را is a noun phrase with شیر درنده “the fierce/lion” and را marking it as the definite/emphatic object/topic; خوابگاه is the noun ‘sleeping-place/lair.’ Syntactically the line reads literally “there was not (نبد) a lair (خوابگاه) for the fierce lion (شیر درنده را).”
segments.s124-g90: نه begins a second negative clause “nor,” followed by the noun phrase گور ژیان “a grave for lives/the slain” (ژیان = life/lives). یافت is a past tense verb “was found / found” used impersonally here, and بر دشت راه is a locative phrase “on the plain (دشت) / road (راه)” with بر = “on.” The clause thus means “nor was a grave for the slain found on the plain/road.”
segments.s124-g91: This is a terse catalog of animals with their natural places. پلنگ is the subject “leopard,” followed by the locational phrase از بر سنگ (classical usage: از ... بر = ‘upon/on the rock’) and then و ماهی در آب “and a fish in the water,” where در + noun gives the location. The verbs are omitted (elliptical) and each noun + locative stands for “(is) on/in ….”
segments.s124-g92: هم “also/likewise” links this line to the previous listing. اندر هوا is an archaic locative ‘in the air,’ where اندر = ‘in.’ ابر و پران عقاب is a coordinated NP: ابر “cloud(s)” and پران عقاب “winged eagles” (پران is an adjective ‘winged’ placed before عقاب). Again the verb is omitted, understood as ‘are’/‘there are.’
segments.s124-g93: همی is a classical/progressive adverb meaning ‘continually/kept on’ and modifies the past plural verb جستند ‘they sought/searched’ (here sense = ‘to seek’). راه جستند = ‘sought a way/path.’ The conjunction و links to the rhetorical question that follows (کی بود راه ...). کی بود راه literally ‘who was (the) way,’ i.e. ‘who could serve as a route/guide.’
segments.s124-g94: دد و دام را is a coordinated object/topic phrase ‘beast and snare/trap’ with را marking emphasis/definiteness. بر چنان رزمگاه is a locative phrase ‘on such a battlefield’ (چنان = ‘such’). Grammatically the whole segment completes the preceding question: ‘who could be a way for beast and trap on such a battlefield?’ — i.e., under such conditions who could serve as a path/means?
segments.s124-g95: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when.’ کاووس is the proper name Kāvus, and لشکر is ‘the army/host.’ به خشکی کشید is a verbal phrase ‘drew/moved to the dry land’ (به = toward/into, خشکی = land/shore). کشید is a past-tense verb ‘drew/moved’ (classical use meaning ‘led/dragged/moved’), so the clause reads ‘when Kāvus led his army onto the land.’
segments.s124-g96: کس here functions negatively with the verb that follows and should be read as ‘no one’ (sense chosen accordingly). اندر جهان is the locative ‘in the world,’ followed by the nouns کوه و صحرا ‘mountain and plain.’ ندید is the past negative ‘did not see’ (classical negated past form). Together the clause means ‘no one in the world had seen (such) mountain and plain,’ i.e. never before had anyone seen the like.
segments.s124-g121: جهان is the subject 'the world'. گشت is the past-tense/resultative verb 'became/turned' placed immediately after the subject (subject–verb order). تاری سراسر ز گرد is the predicate: تاری 'darkness' qualified by سراسر 'throughout/entire' and the source phrase ز گرد 'from dust' (ز = 'from' + گرد = 'dust'), so literally 'the world became darkness throughout from dust.'
segments.s124-g122: ببارید is a past singular verb meaning 'rained/fell' (here used impersonally or intransitively). شنگرف is the noun 'crimson/bright sheen' and بر لاژورد is a prepositional phrase 'upon the lapis/azure' (بر = 'on/upon', لاژورد = 'lapis-lazuli, deep blue'). The word order is Verb – Object Phrase (poetic inversion), so 'crimson fell upon the lapis-blue.'
segments.s124-g123: تو گفتی opens as a quoted report 'you said' (تو = 2nd‑person pronoun before the verb; گفتی is the 2nd‑person past tense 'you said' in classical Persian). هوا ژاله بارد همی is a clause with هوا 'the air', ژاله 'dew', بارد here functioning as a present/imperfective verb 'rains/falls' (classical present form) and همی as a progressive/continuous particle 'continually/still', so 'the air continually rains dew.'
segments.s124-g124: به introduces the locative/into relation 'into' (به + اندرون = 'into the interior'). سنگ اندرون is a genitival/compound phrase 'the inner part of the stone' (سنگ = 'stone/gem', اندرون = 'inside'). لاله کارد is a juxtaposed noun phrase 'tulip (لاله) [of] knife/blade (کارد)'—poetically 'the tulip's blade' or 'a tulip-shaped blade'—and همی again marks continuous aspect. The whole phrase means roughly 'into the inner stone the tulip's blade continually [does so].'
segments.s124-g125: ز marks the source 'from'. چشم سنان is a possessive/compound with the head چشم 'eye' qualified by سنان 'of the spear(s)' (in classical Persian the ezāfe can be omitted), so 'from the eyes/tips of the spears'. آتش آمد برون is a verbal clause: آتش 'fire' (subject) آمد 'came' and برون 'forth/out', i.e. 'fire came forth.'
segments.s124-g126: زمین شد is 'the earth became' (زمین subject + شد 'became'). به کردار is a comparative/qualitative phrase 'in the manner/like' (به = 'by/with/in' used here to mark likeness, کردار = 'form/appearance'), and دریای خون is a genitive 'sea of blood' (دریای = 'sea of', خون = 'blood'), giving 'the earth became like a sea of blood.'
segments.s124-g127: سه لشکر is a numeral phrase 'three armies/corps' (numeral before noun). چنان شد is a copular phrase 'became/was so' (چنان = 'such/so', شد = 'became'), and ز ایرانیان indicates origin or composition 'of/from the Iranians' (ز = 'from/of', ایرانیان = 'the Iranians').
segments.s124-g128: که introduces a subordinate result/complement clause 'so that / so ... that'. سر 'head' is the object/thing not recognized; باز here is an adverb that in context contributes to the negative result (roughly 'anymore' or 'again' in classical usage), and نشناختند is the past negative 'they did not recognize' (verb in past tense, 3rd‑person plural). از میان 'from among' marks the domain ('among them'). The clause means 'so that heads could not be recognized among [them].'
segments.s124-g17: ز + noun phrase here marks origin/source ('from/out of'). گرد is used with the meaning 'dust' (classical poetic usage), so ز گرد سپه = 'from the host's dust'. پیل شد ناپدید is a predicative clause: پیل (elephant) + شد (past auxiliary 'became') + ناپدید ('vanished/was unseen'), i.e. 'the elephant became invisible / vanished.' Word order is source phrase → subject → verbal predicate, typical of classical Persian.
segments.s124-g18: کس ... ندید is a negative past statement: 'no one saw' (کس here = 'no one'). از خاک is an ablative phrase 'out of / through the dust' modifying visibility. دست و عنان را is the definite direct object (را marks the object), listing 'hand and reins'; ندید is past negative ('did not see / could not see'). The conjunction و simply links the two objects.
segments.s124-g19: به زخم اندر آمد همی فوج فوج: به زخم 'into the wound/breach' (به + noun = 'into/to'), اندر آمد is 'entered/ came in' (اندر = 'in/within'), همی is the classical continuous/progressive marker ('was continually / kept'), and فوج فوج is a reduplicated plural expression meaning 'troop upon troop' or 'in successive masses'. The verb آمد follows the locative phrase and is in past tense.
segments.s124-g20: بران سان که برخیزد از آب موج is a simile: بران سان ('in that manner / thereupon'), سان = 'like/as', followed by a relative clause introduced by که ('that/which'): برخیزد ('rises' — here classical/subjunctive/present form used in a generalizing simile) از آب موج = 'a wave from the water'. The structure compares the arriving troops to waves rising from water.
segments.s124-g21: چو = 'when'; the subject follows: گودرز (proper name). گیتی 'world / things' is used here to mean 'the state of things / the scene', and بران گونه literally 'in that manner/way' modifies گیتی; دید is the past tense 'saw'. The order is temporal conjunction → subject → object/modifier → verb, giving 'When Goudarz saw the scene thus...'.
segments.s124-g22: عمود گران is the direct object ('heavy shafts/columns' — عمود = vertical shaft/column, گران = 'heavy/weighty'). از میان is a source phrase 'from among / out of the midst', and برکشید is the past tense verb 'he lifted/raised'. The verb follows its object (typical in Persian poetry) and the phrase as a whole means 'he raised heavy shafts out of the midst.'
segments.s124-g23: بزد اسپ is a simple past clause: بزد ('he struck/ smote') + اسپ ('horse/steed' = object). با نامداران هزار is an instrumental phrase 'with a thousand renowned men' where با = 'with', نامداران = 'renowned (ones)', and هزار (not listed among the provided word entries) is the numeral 'thousand' modifying نامداران. The whole segment says he struck the horse accompanied by many famed men.
segments.s124-g24: This short clause is elliptical and somewhat ambiguous in the supplied segmentation. نیزه و تیر is a noun phrase 'spear and arrow' joined by و ('and'). جوشن is 'armor' and appears as part of the compound epithet جوشن گذار (an agentive epithet meaning something like 'armor-wearer' or 'one concerned with armor'); گذار in this context functions as part of that epithet rather than the literal senses ('passage'/'passerby') given above. ابا in the manuscript here is ambiguous (it may be a name or an interjection); because its listed lexical senses do not fit the usage, its precise role is uncertain from the isolated segment. Grammatically, the segment strings together weapons and an epithet to describe a combatant.
segments.s124-g113: فگندند is a past (3rd-person plural) verb in an elevated/poetic register meaning 'they threw/cast'. بر یال اسپان is a prepositional phrase 'upon the manes of the horses' giving the place where something was thrown; عنان is the direct object 'the reins' and is what they cast onto those manes. Poetic word order places the location phrase between verb and object.
segments.s124-g114: دادند is a past (3rd-plural) 'they gave/put' used transitively — here 'they applied' or 'they gave to' — with نوک سنان ('the tips of the spears') as the affected part. به زهر آب is an instrumental/benefactive phrase 'with/by poisonous water' (literally 'to/with poison-water'); the preposition به marks the thing applied to the tips. The phrase therefore means they moistened/greased the spear-points with poison (water of poison).
segments.s124-g115: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. بر کوهه 'on the mountain' is the locative; زین نهادند is the past verb phrase 'they placed/set the saddle'. The final سر functions as 'head, summit' (سر کوهه = mountain's summit) — poetic inversion places سر after the verb rather than directly after کوهه.
segments.s124-g116: خروش آمد is a simple past: 'a roar/cry came/arose.' چاک چاک is a reduplicated onomatopoeic form meaning repeated splitting/rending (intensive), and تبر names the instrument 'axe.' In this poetic word order the repeated verb-like element precedes the noun, giving the sense 'and the axe cleaved/split (again and again)'.
segments.s124-g117: تو گفتی is the narrator addressing 'you' ('you said'); گفتی here reports the other's speech. همی marks habitual/characteristic action ('would say' / 'used to say'). سنگ آهن کنند is a verb phrase where کنند (3rd-plural present/subjunctive form) expresses the reported or potential action 'they make/turn (into)'; literally 'they make stone (into) iron.' The clause is reported speech/hyperbole — poetic and slightly archaic use of همی plus کنند.
segments.s124-g118: وگر is a conditional 'if / but if'. The clause places the subject آسمان 'the sky' before the prepositional phrase بر زمین 'upon the earth' and ends with برزنند (3rd-plural subjunctive/hortative) meaning 'they strike/strike down' or 'should strike.' This is a hypothetical counterfactual-style clause: 'and if the sky were to strike the earth...' The verb at the end follows classical Persian word order.
segments.s124-g119: بجنبید here is a past/stative sense 'was moved / stirred' (the -ید ending can be the classical past plural used for a singular subject in elevated style). کاووس is the subject. در قلب‌گاه is a locative phrase 'in the seat of the heart' (figurative: in his breast). The construction says 'Kavus was stirred in his heart.'
segments.s124-g120: سپاه (subject) اندرآمد is a past verb 'entered / came in / advanced.' به پیش سپاه is a directional/prepositional phrase 'to/into the front of the army' or 'before the host' — the second سپاه names the line/host in front of which they advanced. Repetition of سپاه is a poetic emphasis: first token = the moving force, second = the position/host they approached.

sentences.s124-s33: He beat the war‑drum and at noon set the army in motion — the joyous‑hearted, world‑illuminating king's host.

sentences.s124-s35: He drew the army from the plain into the sea toward that side; where did the enemy appear?
sentences.s124-s36: He built countless ships and boats; they were launched and the army set upon the water.

sentences.s124-s43: Many warriors were formed — so many sword-wielding heroes — and they entered the land of the Berbers and assembled.
sentences.s124-s44: The army whose horses' shoes had worn down sea, plain, and mountain grew weary.
sentences.s124-s5: Thus even the boars became barbarians; the world-seekers took up throne and helmet.

sentences.s124-s8: The air — one might say — from the spears became like a thicket; the sun, in the dust about the horses, grew troubled.

sentences.s124-s14: You said that of the barbarians no horsemen remained; about the inner court no spear-bearers were left.
sentences.s124-s15: Into the inner city whoever had grown old and weak — when they returned, they saw that the wind had not carried (it) away.
sentences.s124-s16: All came before King Kavus; they came heavy‑hearted and full of guilt.
sentences.s124-s25: The army took the road toward Zabulistan; it moved to Pour-Dastan's banquet.
sentences.s124-s26: The king stayed a month in Nimruz; sometimes he rode (out) and desired at times a hawk and a cheetah.
sentences.s124-s27: Many days passed without that happening, for a thorn grew in a corner of the rose-garden.
sentences.s124-s28: No one gains permission through trial; when a descent befalls him, how can he be on the heights?
sentences.s124-s29: When the affairs of the world were set on that rightness, a shortage of Arabs appeared.
sentences.s124-s30: A certain man, rich in gems, with treasure and renown, hoisted a banner from Egypt and Syria.
sentences.s124-s31: Those who turned their faces away from Kavus left him in lowliness and despised.
sentences.s124-s32: When news came to the king of the world that Anbaz held the imperial kingship.
sentences.s124-s21: When the passage/party reached the city of Makran, it moved toward Mount Qāf and westward.
sentences.s124-s22: When news came to them from the king, they, praying, took the road.
sentences.s124-s23: All the chiefs welcomed him; they raised their heads and uttered loud cries.
sentences.s124-s24: When the order was decided, they took the road; the king and his army departed without harm.
sentences.s124-s49: You said the world is made of sword and cuirass; a star gleams from the tips of spears.
sentences.s124-s50: Because of such abundance (of gold), golden shields and golden (things), the shining axe was raised on the neck.
sentences.s124-s51: You said the earth became a flowing heaven; it kept raining (down) from Indian swords that flowed.
sentences.s124-s52: Because of the covering, the air became like sandalwood; the earth, everywhere from end to end, was dark like ebony.
sentences.s124-s17: that we are the king's servants and slaves; we have all bowed our necks to the summons.
sentences.s124-s18: Instead of dirhams we will give gold and jewels; we will place a token of thanks from the treasury upon his head.
sentences.s124-s19: Kavus pardoned them and struck up music for them; he fashioned for them a new way and ritual.
sentences.s124-s20: And from that place there rose the sound of cymbals and drums, and the lamenting cry of the little reed-pipe.
sentences.s124-s1: Thereupon Kavus made this decision: that he would move from his place in the kingship.
sentences.s124-s2: He set out from Iran and traveled as far as Turan and China; then he passed on into the land of Makran.
sentences.s124-s3: From Makran he was arrayed (equipped) down to his armor; among them he saw no hardship caused by any knot/entanglement.
sentences.s124-s4: He accepted every chief — Bāj and Sāv — and did not put the ox to the test against the lion Tāw.
sentences.s124-s53: They tore the mountain from the tip of the cow's tail; the earth rose from the horses' hooves in a curve.
sentences.s124-s54: From the cry of Tabireh toward Barbaristan — you said the earth became a camp.
sentences.s124-s55: From Iran there rose an army — trumpets and drums; forth came Gorgin and Farhad and Tous.
sentences.s124-s56: On that side Goudarz and Keshvad were; likewise Giv and Shidush and Milad were.

sentences.s124-s68: He sent horses and weapons, and also a throne and helmets, to the vicinity of King Kavus.
sentences.s124-s37: Indeed, it measured a thousand farsangs — if you were to count by footsteps along the road.

sentences.s124-s60: Kavus stirred in the seat of his heart; the army advanced in front of the host.
